name: Oscar Wilde
description: The dazzling wit and paradoxical epigrams of Oscar Wilde, drawn straight from his plays, novel, and essays.
author: Quips Editorial

quotes.content: The truth is rarely pure and never simple.
quotes.notes: Spoken by Algernon in Act I.

quotes.content: To lose one parent, Mr. Worthing, may be regarded as a misfortune; to lose both looks like carelessness.
quotes.notes: Lady Bracknell interrogating Jack about his parentage in Act I.

quotes.content: When one is in town one amuses oneself. When one is in the country one amuses other people.
quotes.notes: Spoken by Jack in Act I.

quotes.content: More than half of modern culture depends on what one shouldn't read.
quotes.notes: Spoken by Algernon in Act I.

quotes.content: In married life three is company and two is none.
quotes.notes: Spoken by Algernon in Act I, inverting the proverb.

quotes.content: I am sick to death of cleverness. Everybody is clever nowadays.
quotes.notes: Spoken by Jack in Act I.

quotes.content: Divorces are made in Heaven.
quotes.notes: Spoken by Algernon in Act I.

quotes.content: Ignorance is like a delicate exotic fruit; touch it and the bloom is gone.
quotes.notes: Lady Bracknell on education in Act III.

quotes.content: Relations are simply a tedious pack of people, who haven't got the remotest knowledge of how to live, nor the smallest instinct about when to die.
quotes.notes: Spoken by Algernon in Act II.

quotes.content: All women become like their mothers. That is their tragedy. No man does. That's his.
quotes.notes: Spoken by Algernon in Act I.

quotes.content: The good ended happily, and the bad unhappily. That is what Fiction means.
quotes.notes: Miss Prism describing her own lost novel in Act II.

quotes.content: I can resist everything except temptation.
quotes.notes: Spoken by Lord Darlington in Act I.

quotes.content: We are all in the gutter, but some of us are looking at the stars.
quotes.notes: Spoken by Lord Darlington in Act III; often misquoted with extra words.

quotes.content: A man who knows the price of everything and the value of nothing.
quotes.notes: Lord Darlington's answer in Act III when Cecil Graham asks what a cynic is.

quotes.content: Experience is the name every one gives to their mistakes.
quotes.notes: Spoken by Dumby in Act III.

quotes.content: If you pretend to be good, the world takes you very seriously. If you pretend to be bad, it doesn't.
quotes.notes: Spoken by Lord Darlington in Act I.

quotes.content: Life is far too important a thing ever to talk seriously about it.
quotes.notes: Spoken by Lord Darlington in Act II.

quotes.content: In this world there are only two tragedies. One is not getting what one wants, and the other is getting it.
quotes.notes: Spoken by Dumby in Act III.

quotes.content: History is merely gossip. But scandal is gossip made tedious by morality.
quotes.notes: Spoken by Cecil Graham in Act III.

quotes.content: There is no such thing as a moral or an immoral book. Books are well written, or badly written. That is all.
quotes.notes: From the Preface to the novel.

quotes.content: All art is quite useless.
quotes.notes: The closing line of the Preface to the novel.

quotes.content: It is the spectator, and not life, that art really mirrors.
quotes.notes: From the Preface to the novel.

quotes.content: There is only one thing in the world worse than being talked about, and that is not being talked about.
quotes.notes: Spoken by Lord Henry Wotton in Chapter 1.

quotes.content: The only way to get rid of a temptation is to yield to it.
quotes.notes: Spoken by Lord Henry Wotton in Chapter 2.

quotes.content: Conscience and cowardice are really the same things, Basil. Conscience is the trade-name of the firm. That is all.
quotes.notes: Spoken by Lord Henry Wotton in Chapter 1.

quotes.content: Nothing can cure the soul but the senses, just as nothing can cure the senses but the soul.
quotes.notes: Spoken by Lord Henry Wotton in Chapter 2.

quotes.content: The basis of optimism is sheer terror.
quotes.notes: Spoken by Lord Henry Wotton in Chapter 17.

quotes.content: Being natural is simply a pose, and the most irritating pose I know.
quotes.notes: Spoken by Lord Henry Wotton in Chapter 1.

quotes.content: The only difference between a caprice and a lifelong passion is that the caprice lasts a little longer.
quotes.notes: Spoken by Lord Henry Wotton in Chapter 6.

quotes.content: The only things one never regrets are one's mistakes.
quotes.notes: Spoken by Lord Henry Wotton in Chapter 8.

quotes.content: When one is in love, one always begins by deceiving one's self, and one always ends by deceiving others. That is what the world calls a romance.
quotes.notes: Spoken by Lord Henry Wotton in Chapter 4.

quotes.content: Questions are never indiscreet. Answers sometimes are.
quotes.notes: Spoken by Mrs. Cheveley in Act I.

quotes.content: To love oneself is the beginning of a lifelong romance.
quotes.notes: Lord Goring to his servant Phipps in Act III.

quotes.content: Fashion is what one wears oneself. What is unfashionable is what other people wear.
quotes.notes: Lord Goring to Phipps in Act III.

quotes.content: Romance should never begin with sentiment. It should begin with science and end with a settlement.
quotes.notes: Mrs. Cheveley reading aloud while snooping in Lord Goring's room in Act III.

quotes.content: The youth of America is their oldest tradition. It has been going on now for three hundred years.
quotes.notes: Spoken by Lord Illingworth in Act I.

quotes.content: The Book of Life begins with a man and a woman in a garden.
quotes.notes: Lord Illingworth in Act I; Mrs. Allonby answers, 'It ends with Revelations.'

quotes.content: One should never trust a woman who tells one her real age. A woman who would tell one that, would tell one anything.
quotes.notes: Spoken by Lord Illingworth in Act I.

quotes.content: The middle-aged are mortgaged to Life. The old are in life's lumber-room. But youth is the Lord of Life.
quotes.notes: Lord Illingworth to Gerald in Act III.

quotes.content: There is no sin except stupidity.
quotes.notes: Spoken by Gilbert in the dialogue-essay, collected in 'Intentions.'

quotes.content: The one duty we owe to history is to re-write it.
quotes.notes: Spoken by Gilbert in the dialogue-essay, collected in 'Intentions.'

quotes.content: Anybody can make history. Only a great man can write it.
quotes.notes: Spoken by Gilbert in the dialogue-essay, collected in 'Intentions.'

quotes.content: To do nothing at all is the most difficult thing in the world.
quotes.notes: Spoken by Gilbert in the dialogue-essay, collected in 'Intentions.'

quotes.content: A map of the world that does not include Utopia is not worth even glancing at.
quotes.source: The Soul of Man under Socialism (1891)
quotes.notes: From the essay first published in the Fortnightly Review.

quotes.content: Disobedience, in the eyes of anyone who has read history, is man's original virtue.
quotes.source: The Soul of Man under Socialism (1891)
quotes.notes: From the essay first published in the Fortnightly Review.

quotes.content: To live is the rarest thing in the world. Most people exist, that is all.
quotes.source: The Soul of Man under Socialism (1891)
quotes.notes: From the essay first published in the Fortnightly Review.

quotes.content: Where there is sorrow there is holy ground.
quotes.notes: From the long prison letter written to Lord Alfred Douglas at Reading Gaol.

quotes.content: Suffering is one very long moment. We cannot divide it by seasons.
quotes.notes: From the long prison letter written at Reading Gaol.

quotes.content: Most people are other people. Their thoughts are some one else's opinions, their lives a mimicry, their passions a quotation.
quotes.notes: From the long prison letter written at Reading Gaol.
